bids.reasoning: Since the negotiation is just beginning (t=0), I will start by offering my maximum utility bid to set a strong anchor. I am proposing my most preferred values for all issues, which gives me a utility of 1.0. I will monitor the opponent's counter-offers to find mutually beneficial trade-offs later.

The opponent has made an extreme initial offer accompanied by an early threat, which I treat as standard cheap talk for this stage of the negotiation (t=0.02). To demonstrate a willingness to negotiate without yielding to their threats, I will make a minor concession on my least important issue (issueA) while holding firm on my most critical issues (issueC and issueD) to keep my overall utility very high.

The opponent is employing an aggressive early hardball tactic, repeating a completely self-serving offer and sending intimidating messages without actually making any concessions. Since the time fraction is only 0.04, there is no real time pressure to capitulate. I will continue to explore potential compromises by substituting my previous concession on issueA with a slight concession on issueD, maintaining a very high utility for myself while attempting to find some traction.

The opponent is repeating their opening offer and making aggressive threats, which are simply cheap talk tactics to create fake urgency this early in the negotiation (t=0.06). I will ignore these threats and continue making small concessions to see if they are willing to engage constructively. I will concede slightly on issueA, which has the lowest weight for me, while maintaining high values on my most important issues (issueC and issueD).

The current time is very early (t=0.08), giving me ample room to negotiate despite the opponent's aggressive threats and demands for immediate capitulation. These threats are classic cheap talk aiming to rush an acceptance of an offer that yields my lowest possible utility score (0.20). I will continue to hold firm on my most highly valued issues (B, C, D) but make a moderate concession on issueC to valueC to signal my willingness to find a compromise, maintaining an overall utility above 0.82.

The opponent is aggressively repeating the same offer while using cheap talk threats ("I walk away", "final position") at a very early stage of the negotiation (t=0.1). I will completely ignore these non-binding threats and fake urgency. I will continue making incremental concessions, particularly on my lowest-weighted issue (issueA), while holding firm on my most important issues (issueC and issueD) to keep my utility above 0.80.

The opponent has finally shown a willingness to concede by moving on issueC, contradicting their threats to walk away. Given the low time fraction (t=0.12), I can safely ignore their aggressive cheap talk and continue making measured, incremental concessions. I will reciprocate their movement by conceding slightly on issueB (from valueF to valueE, holding steady on other issues) to maintain a high utility of roughly 0.75 while showing good faith.

The negotiation is still in the early stages (t=0.14), so there is plenty of time to negotiate despite the opponent's repeated "final offer" and "walk away" threats, which are standard aggressive tactics. The opponent has started making small concessions by moving to valueB on issueC, indicating a willingness to negotiate. I will continue to make measured concessions on my least important issue (issueA) while holding firm on my most highly weighted issues (issueC and issueD) to keep my target utility around 0.73.

The opponent is aggressively holding their position while sending heavy threats, but it is still very early in the negotiation (t=0.16). They have only made a marginal concession on issueC, which suggests they are testing my boundaries. I will fully concede on my lowest-weighted issue (issueA) to their preferred value to signal cooperation, but continue holding firm on my high-value issues (issueB, issueC, and issueD) to protect my utility.

The opponent continues to bid aggressively and rigidly at an incredibly low utility for me, using hostile tactics and fake urgency despite it being very early in the negotiation (t=0.18). I have already conceded entirely on my least important issue (issueA) to show good faith. I will now offer another compromise on my second-lowest priority (issueB), while holding firm on my most highly weighted issues (issueC and issueD) to keep my overall utility healthy at ~0.69.

The opponent is using highly aggressive language and fake urgency, but we are only at t=0.20. While they are heavily anchored on `valueA` for most issues, they have slowly started conceding on `issueC` (moving from `valueA` to `valueB` to `valueD`). Since `issueC` and `issueD` are my most important issues, I will hold firm on high-utility values for them (`valueC` and `valueE` respectively) while making a minor concession on `issueB` (from `valueB` to `valueD`) to keep the negotiation progressing without sacrificing much overall utility.

The opponent has been using aggressive language in their messages, demanding I accept their offers immediately, but their actual bidding history shows them making gradual concessions (moving to issueC: valueB, then valueD, and most recently issueA: valueB). I will ignore their cheap talk regarding "final meaningful concessions" and focus on their bid data. Since it is still early (t=0.22), I will accept their latest shift on issueA (valueB) to show cooperation, while proposing a new value for issueB and holding firm on issues C and D, targeting an overall utility around 0.70.

The opponent has engaged in aggressive cheap talk while simultaneously making a very favorable concession on issueC (moving to valueC, which grants me 0.93 utility). I will ignore their empty threats of walking away, as the deadline is still distant (t=0.24). I will lock in their concession on issueC and their preferred valueA on issueA (my lowest-weighted issue), while continuing to hold firm on issueD and proposing a new moderate option (valueC) on issueB to find a workable middle ground.

The opponent's aggressive messages and repeated threats to walk away are transparent cheap talk given it's early in the negotiation (t=0.26). However, analyzing their bidding history shows they have occasionally deviated from 'valueA' across different rounds (proposing valueB for issueA, valueB for issueB, and valueD for issueC). I will combine these three opponent-suggested values into a single offer to test their actual flexibility, while returning issueD to my top preference ('valueF') to maintain a solid overall utility of ~0.67. This demonstrates cooperation without ceding unnecessary overall value.

The opponent's messages are highly aggressive, consistently threatening to walk away, yet their actual bids show a pattern of gradual concessions across all issues (exploring valueB and valueC on issues B, C, and D). Treating the threats as cheap talk given the early time fraction (t=0.28), I will counter by adopting values they have previously proposed for issues B and C, while holding firm on values that yield high utility for me on issues C and D. I will offer issueA: valueA, issueB: valueB, issueC: valueC, issueD: valueD, which hits a respectable utility of ~0.65 for me.

The opponent is using repetitive aggressive cheap talk (threatening to walk away every round), but their offers show slow but steady concessions (like issueB: valueD and issueD: valueC recently). I will ignore the emotional appeals and threats. As it is only t=0.3, I will match their latest concession on issueB (valueD) while continuing to concede on issueA, but I will firmly protect my utilities on my highest-weight issues, C and D. This brings my target utility to roughly 0.68.

The opponent's repetitive aggressive messages ("final move", "walk away") contrast sharply with their ongoing, incremental concessions across different issues (offering issueC: valueC, issueB: valueD, and issueD: valueD). I am largely ignoring their threats as cheap talk and focusing on the underlying shifts in their offers. To keep the process moving at t=0.32, I will propose a composite offer that incorporates several of their recent concessions while retaining high utility values for my priority issues.

The opponent's repeated threats to walk away are standard cheap-talk negotiation tactics. However, I can observe from their bidding history that they are methodically exploring various options across issues, particularly proposing `valueB` for issueA, `valueB` and `valueD` for issueB, `valueB` and `valueC` for issueC, and `valueD` for issueD. To make progress while keeping my utility solid (~0.65), I will combine several of these previously explored values, yielding on issueA and matching their past bids on issueB, issueC, and issueD, which are more important to me.

The opponent has repeatedly threatened to walk away since the beginning of the negotiation, which I recognize as empty threats and cheap talk. I will ignore the bluffs and continue methodically finding a mutually agreeable package. I am incorporating their most recent concessions on issueA and issueB, while insisting on values for issueC and issueD that they have previously proposed and which maintain a reasonable utility for me (around 0.64).

The opponent continues to make repetitive threats and exaggerated claims of "final offers," which contradict their behavior of making small concessions on issues B, C, and D over time. Since we are only at t=0.38, I will ignore their cheap talk. They have recently offered valueC on issueB and valueC on issueC, so I will match those to find common ground, while securing high utility for myself by standing firm on valueE for my most important issue (issueD) and conceding valueA on the lowest-weight issue (issueA).

The opponent's messages rely on repetitive hard-bargaining threats which I will ignore, relying instead on their actual bidding behavior. Their recent offers show a willingness to concede on my lower-weight issues (issueA: valueB, issueB: valueC). I will match these specific values to demonstrate responsiveness, while holding firm on my most important issues (issueC: valueC, issueD: valueE) to maintain an overall utility near 0.70.

The opponent's intense and repetitive "final offer" messages correspond to a strategy of cheap talk, as they are actively adjusting their proposals (e.g., offering issueC: valueD and issueD: valueC recently). With time at 0.42, I am conceding further on my lower-weight issues (issueA to 'valueA' and issueB to 'valueB') and even moving to their newly proposed 'valueD' on issueC. In return, I am holding firm on 'valueE' for my highest-weight issueD, maintaining a balanced mid-game utility of around 0.65.

The opponent's aggressive messages repeatedly threaten to walk away while claiming their offers are "final," yet their bidding history shows continuous concessions, confirming their threats are merely cheap talk. At t=0.44, they conceded to issueC=valueC and issueD=valueB. I will synthesize a new offer by combining values they have independently proposed across recent rounds (issueA=valueB, issueB=valueD, issueC=valueC, issueD=valueD). This yields a solid utility for me (~0.65) while signaling genuine compromise by strictly using values they have demonstrated a willingness to accept.

The opponent's repetitive messages about "final offers" and "walking away" conflict with their continued bidding and shifting values, so I will treat them as cheap talk. We are near the midpoint (t=0.46), so I can afford to assemble a bundle entirely from values they have recently offered across different turns: issueB=valueD (from t=0.30), issueC=valueC (from t=0.44), and issueD=valueD (from t=0.32). This protects my core utilities while presenting a package they have effectively signaled is within their acceptable range.

The current time fraction is reaching mid-game (0.48), despite the opponent's aggressive threats to walk away, which are classic cheap talk. The opponent's offers have shifted across multiple values for issues C and D, while remaining fixed primarily on valueA/valueB for issueA and valueA/valueB for issueB. I will concede further by providing `valueA` and `valueB` on the low-priority issues (issueA and issueB), but I will anchor on `valueC` and `valueD` for my high-priority issues (issueC and issueD) to maintain a utility of ~0.64.

The opponent's messages constantly threaten to walk away and claim their offers are "final," yet their bids keep changing every round, indicating these threats are cheap talk and they are still willing to negotiate. At the halfway point (t=0.5), I will propose a bid that incorporates several values the opponent has recently proposed (issueA: valueA, issueB: valueB, and issueC: valueC), while insisting on a high-utility value for my most important issue (issueD: valueE). This maintains my overall utility near 0.69, which is appropriate for the mid-game phase.

The time fraction is 0.52, so we are well into the mid-game. I am targeting an offer that preserves utility around 0.65 while incorporating values the opponent has signaled a willingness to accept in recent rounds (issueA: valueB, issueB: valueB, issueD: valueD). The opponent's message history is highly repetitive and relies on aggressive threats to walk away, which I recognize as cheap talk since they keep returning with varied offers.

The opponent has shown significant flexibility on `issueD` over recent turns, most recently proposing `valueE`, which provides me very high utility (0.95). In turn, they have previously offered `valueC` on `issueC` and `valueB` on `issueB`. I will construct an offer that captures their concession on `issueD` while continuing to propose values they have indicated a willingness to accept on other issues, maintaining utility around 0.69. I am ignoring their aggressive communication tactics, as their actual bidding behavior clearly demonstrates they are still willing to negotiate.

I note your repeated aggressive messages about walking away, but the actual history of your offers indicates flexibility across various issues. You recently proposed `valueD` for `issueA` and `issueC`, `valueC` for `issueB`, and `valueE` for `issueD`. I am constructing a composite offer from these exact values, providing a mutually acceptable configuration that still yields me strong utility while directly responding to your recent choices.

The opponent's threatening language is consistently aggressive but contradicts their actual bidding behavior, which shows minor concessions across different issues. In their last few offers, they have proposed 'valueA' for issueA, 'valueF' for issueB, 'valueD' for issueC, and 'valueE' for issueD. Since all of these values have appeared in their recent offers and provide me with a solid utility of ~0.74, I am combining them to signal a willingness to agree on mutually proposed terms while protecting my high-weight utility.

The opponent continues to use aggressive cheap talk and threats of walking away, but their actual offers show movement, recently proposing valueE for issueD, which gives me near-maximum utility (0.95). I will match their latest concessions on issueA (valueB) and issueD (valueE). For issueB, I will return to valueF, which the opponent was willing to offer at t=0.58, and for issueC, I'll propose valueC, keeping my overall utility strong (~0.79) as we move into the second half of the negotiation.

The opponent continues to use aggressive "take it or leave it" language, which I will ignore as standard cheap talk. Their offers, however, show shifting concessions: they've proposed `issueB: valueF` and `issueC: valueB` recently, as well as `issueD: valueE` in the prior round. I will combine these previously offered values (`issueA: valueB`, `issueB: valueF`, `issueC: valueB`, `issueD: valueE`) to create a package that yields ~0.72 utility for me while assembling concessions they have already signaled are feasible for them.

The opponent's repetitive aggressive messages are cheap talk, but their actual bids reveal an occasional willingness to concede on high-value items for me, such as proposing issueC as valueF and issueB as valueD recently, along with past offers of issueD as valueE. Given we are at t=0.64, I will merge these revealed acceptable values into a package offering. This combination targets a solid utility of ~0.71 while showing flexibility and directly incorporating values they have newly proposed.

The opponent continues to rely on cheap-talk aggressiveness while actively making concessions, such as offering B: F, D: C, and A: A on recent turns, and even C: F at t=0.64. I will anchor on the values they have shown willingness to accept (A: A, B: F) while pushing back on issues C and D to maintain my utility above 0.75. This allows me to propose a strong tradeoff without being influenced by their repetitive threats to walk away.

The opponent's messages consists of rigid, repetitive threats, but their actual offers show slight movement across all issues, providing opportunities to find common ground. Their last few offers included concessions on issueB (valueF), issueC (valueD), and issueA (valueB). To maximize negotiations and move closer to an agreement before the deadline, I will construct a bid that aggregates the opponent's previous concessions (issueA: valueB, issueB: valueF, issueC: valueD) while holding steady on issueD (valueD). This yields a utility of approximately 0.71 for me while aligning with values they have demonstrated a willingness to accept.

The opponent has signaled flexibility on issues B and C, recently proposing valueF for issueB and valueC for issueC, which afford me high utilities. I will accommodate their strong preference on issueA by conceding to valueA. Meanwhile, I will propose valueD for issueD to maintain my utility around 0.74, a value they have previously proposed in other configurations, moving us closer to an agreement without yielding entirely to their most demanding terms.

The opponent's offers have been widely varied, but in round t=0.70 they proposed a combination we almost completely agreed on (`issueA: valueA, issueB: valueF, issueC: valueC`), differing only on `issueD` where they wanted `valueB` and I proposed `valueD`. To advance the negotiation, I will propose exactly those three shared values and concede slightly on `issueD` to `valueC` (which the opponent recently offered at t=0.66). This maintains a strong utility of approximately 0.68 for me while directly addressing their previously stated preferences.
